The chief complaint is unsteady gait; review of xrays Subjective This is a 77-year-old Filipino male with a past medical history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes, GERD, BPH, mild neuropathy, arthritis in both hips and knees, seasonal allergies, a history of meningitis, and a brain aneurysm, seen for a routine follow-up today. Xrays from previous fall reviewed: negative for fractures, does have osteopenia He continues to have generalized weakness, discomfort in both knees, bilateral hips, and an unsteady gait. Reports continued bilateral lower extremity weakness. He is not using his cane at all times for assistance in ambulation. Denies any recent falls. Patient is status post lung resection for mediastinal mass and discharged from Saint Rose San Martin on 5/14/2025. Patient seen by Dr. Park for follow-up visit. Patient does have continue to have occasional dry cough but much improved since starting on Budesonide/ Formoterol HFA 2 puffs twice daily. ENT Dr Salinas follow-up March 2026, pulmonology followup with Dr Flores March 2026 Patient is reported to doze off occasionally during the day and constantly feels tired Patient has had a sleep study in Alaska and declines any additional sleep studies for now. He does admit to constantly thinking about things at bedtime and unable to stop. PHQ-2 2 points No fever, headache, n/v, SOB, or chest tightness. Patient reports feeling of incomplete bladder emptying and frequent urination. No cloudy or foul smelling urine, no hematuria, no abdominal fullness or dysuria. No urology appointments made. Information given to patient to scheduled for an appointment. Seen by cardiologist for annual exam, reports all tests done have been good. His recent history includes a lung resection for a mediastinal mass, which necessitates ongoing assessment and support for respiratory management, as evidenced by his chronic cough and recent use of inhalers. The patient is considered homebound as he has significant painful degenerative arthritis of both hips and knees and osteopenia limiting weight-bearing tolerance, and generalized fatigue and muscle weakness that substantially impair transfers and sustained ambulation. These combined impairments make leaving the home unsafe and require a considerable and taxing effort: ambulation outside the home provokes marked fatigue, increases fall risk due to lower-extremity weakness and gait instability, and raises the potential for respiratory compromise after thoracic surgery. Allowable absences are limited to medically necessary appointments and supervised therapy visits only. These documented functional limitations, comorbid conditions, and recent post-operative respiratory status objectively support Medicare homebound criteria and medically justify the need for skilled nursing for medication management, post-operative and respiratory assessment, and skilled physical therapy to improve strength, gait safety, and reduce fall risk. The effort required to leave home is considerable, as any attempt to do so results in exhaustion, necessitating extended rest periods for recovery. A head-to-toe assessment reveals that Danilo has compromised cardiopulmonary status, experiencing dyspnea when walking more than 20 feet or climbing stairs. Gastrointestinal assessment shows he has no significant issues, and his integumentary assessment indicates no open wounds or pressure ulcers. His safety is a concern due to impaired balance, which increases his risk of falling. Vital signs taken during the assessment showed a temperature of 97.3°F, an apical pulse of 67 beats per minute, a blood pressure of 116/68 mmHg, and a respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute. These vital signs indicate that Danilo's overall physiological status is stable, albeit necessitating close monitoring due to his underlying health conditions. Danilo's activities of daily living (ADLs) have been significantly affected by his osteoarthritis. He primarily requires assistance with dressing, grooming, and bathing. He is dependent on others for supervision or minimal assistance during these activities. However, he is independent in some aspects of self-care, such as feeding himself, provided he receives setup assistance. His functional ability is categorized as needing assistance for indoor ambulation and accessing stairs, he requires an assistive device for mobility. Danilo's mobility is limited, as he can walk with the aid of a one-handed device but has difficulties negotiating uneven surfaces. His living situation presents social needs that may require further support, especially given the limited capabilities of his non-agency caregiver. In conclusion, there is a medical necessity for skilled services in Danilo's home health care plan due to his multiple comorbidities and the need for ongoing monitoring and assistance with ADLs. The recommended visit frequency should be at least two to three times per week to ensure adequate support and management of his health conditions, facilitate rehabilitation, and enhance his quality of life while maintaining safety and independence.
